description: The bird was foraging along the roadside, the only area not snow-covered, with several dozen Horned Larks. I first noticed the Chestnut-collared Longspur when it flew a few feet and showed its significant white retrices as it landed. Jessica and I were then both on the bird, and noted its nearly all-black belly. In profile, the reddish nape was obvious. It did not yet have well-defined facial markings, either black streaks or yellow patch. We were able to watch it forage for about 20 seconds before it again flushed a short distance and called. It gave the distinctive "cher-dee" flight call of Chestnut-collared Longspur twice from about twelve feet in the air, and then landed again. I took one photograph about this time (included and uncropped). The bird is in flight, pointed away from the camera, and blurry, but the white retrices and part of its black belly are still visible. Just after the photo, two other cars drove by, flushing the entire flock of Horned Larks and the Chestnut-collared Longspur. We did not locate the bird again after that.
